title: T/F/NI + Vocabulary: A Career in Food Science

tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1.–1.6.) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie znajdz w tekscie wyrazy lub wyrazenia, ktore odpowiadaja definicjom podanym w punktach 1.7.–1.11. Wymagana jest calkowita poprawnosc ortograficzna odpowiedzi.
tasks.text:
HOW I BUILT A CAREER IN FOOD SCIENCE

I always say that I had my first job before I was ten years old: taste tester in my grandmother's kitchen. She was a legendary cook in our neighbourhood, and I spent every weekend perched on her kitchen counter, sampling sauces and suggesting — with the authority of a small child — what was missing. Looking back, those early experiences planted a seed that would take many years to grow.

At school, I excelled in chemistry and biology, but it was not until I read a magazine article about food technology in my early teens that I realised science and food could be combined into a profession. The idea captivated me. I enrolled in a food science degree at a university in my home city, which turned out to be one of the best decisions I have ever made.

My first job after graduation was at a large food manufacturing company. The work was interesting but the environment was intensely competitive. I had to fight hard to make my voice heard at meetings, and there were several occasions when ideas I had put forward were quietly adopted by senior colleagues without any acknowledgement. I considered leaving more than once, but I told myself I had to pay my dues — that building a career takes time and setbacks are part of the process.

The opportunity that changed everything came when a small start-up reached out to me. They were working on plant-based proteins and needed someone with expertise in food texture. It was a huge leap into the unknown. The salary was lower, the company was tiny, and there was no guarantee of long-term security. But within two years, we had developed a product that was picked up by a major supermarket chain, and the company grew almost overnight.

I am often asked what the key to success in food science is, and my honest answer is curiosity. The most important skill is not knowing all the answers, but knowing how to ask the right questions. Every failed experiment is a piece of data. Every complaint from a consumer is a gift. The work is never finished, and that is precisely what keeps me coming back.

items.statement: The narrator first realised that science and food could form a career while still at primary school.
items.explanation: FALSE — The text says 'it was not until I read a magazine article about food technology in my early teens', so the realisation came during secondary school, not primary.

items.statement: The narrator's grandmother worked professionally as a cook.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — The text describes her as 'a legendary cook in our neighbourhood' but says nothing about whether cooking was her profession.

items.statement: At the manufacturing company, the narrator's ideas were sometimes used by others without credit.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'Ideas I had put forward were quietly adopted by senior colleagues without any acknowledgement'.

items.statement: The narrator's salary increased when moving to the start-up.
items.explanation: FALSE — 'The salary was lower' at the start-up.

items.statement: The product developed at the start-up was sold through a major supermarket.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'We had developed a product that was picked up by a major supermarket chain'.

items.statement: According to the narrator, failed experiments provide useful information.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'Every failed experiment is a piece of data'.

vocabItems.definition: to be very good or highly successful at something
to attract and hold someone's complete interest and attention
to go through hardships and difficulties in order to earn success or experience
a situation that involves doing something completely new, with no certainty of what will happen
official recognition given to someone for their contribution or work
